bnb.caveat: PROVISIONAL: every 'bnb' config in this sweep (n=2) was undamaged - there is no damaged config to set a two-sided boundary from. tau is placed at the lowest observed (undamaged) d_comm, which means the boundary-exact config certifies with zero margin ('>=', not '>') - measurement noise on a config sitting at exactly this d_comm could flip its verdict. Measure at least one damaged config in this family to firm this up.

gptq.caveat: PROVISIONAL: every 'gptq' config in this sweep (n=3) showed real downstream damage - there is no undamaged config to set a two-sided safe floor from. tau is a lower bound consistent with the data (placed just above the worst observed damage), not a measured safe/unsafe boundary. Any tau above the worst-damage point in this sweep is equally consistent with it - measure at least one undamaged config in this family to firm this up.

caveat: d_comm is not comparable across quantization methods, so tau is calibrated independently per method_family — see each family's own 'caveat' for its specific confidence level. Pooling families into one tau produces a false-safe when an undamaged config from one family sits below a damaged config from another on the pooled axis (see paper §3.4 / SPEC.md).
note: cos_sims reconstructed from the exact d_comm values stored in the shipped certificates (cert_int8.json, cert_nf4.json, cert_gptq_int4/3/2.json) via the inverse of Eq.1 (d_comm is a bijective function of cosine similarity in the unclamped regime, [0.95,1.0] here) -- raw cos_sims arrays were not persisted to disk at capture time, only their derived d_comm. Reproduces the shipped d_comm to 4 decimal places for every config.

bnb.reason: both bnb configs certify safe under either tau (both d_comm > both thresholds)

gptq.reason: all three gptq configs certify unsafe under either tau (all d_comm < both thresholds)
